error.signature: AI tells a traveler that Egypt entry is free or that visa on arrival has no conditions

verdict.summary: Most visitors to Egypt need a visa. Many nationalities can buy a visa on arrival (around USD 25) at major airports or apply for an e-Visa in advance via the official portal. Rules vary by nationality and entry point, and visitors to South Sinai resorts may get a limited free entry permit that does not allow travel elsewhere.

dead_ends.action: Assume Egypt is visa-free, or that visa on arrival is available at every entry point for every nationality
dead_ends.why_fails: Visa requirements depend on nationality and entry point; not all borders sell visa on arrival and some nationalities must arrange a visa in advance. Assuming free entry risks refusal.
dead_ends.common_misconception: VOA availability and free entry are not universal across nationalities and borders.

dead_ends.action: Rely on the South Sinai free entry permit for a Nile or Cairo trip
dead_ends.why_fails: The visa-free Sinai-only permit (about 15 days) is limited to South Sinai resort areas and does NOT allow travel to Cairo, Luxor, or diving outside the zone - you need a full visa for those.

workarounds.action: Apply for the e-Visa on the official portal before travel, or buy visa on arrival at a major airport if eligible.
workarounds.how: Use only visa2egypt.gov.eg; carry USD cash as a VOA backup; confirm your nationality's rule first.

workarounds.action: For a full-country trip, get a proper tourist visa rather than the Sinai-only permit.
workarounds.how: Choose the full tourist visa if you plan to leave South Sinai.
workarounds.tradeoff: A full visa costs more than the free Sinai permit.
